coredata.dc:title: Construction of porous poly (l-lactic acid) surface via carbon quantum dots-assisted static Breath-Figures method
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yunfeng
Sun, Xin
Wang, Rui
Tang, Hui
Wang, Lixin
Zhang, Lihui
Qin, Shuhao
coredata.dc:description: Focused on the design and construction of surface porous morphology of poly (L-lactic acid) membrane. A bottom-up surface patterning technique, static Breath-Figures method, combined with Pickering emulsion mechanism is regulated to fabricate porous poly (L-lactic acid) membrane surface rapidly and reliably. Carbon quantum dots, synthesized in an environment-respecting route, are used to assist the formation of droplet templates. The dimensions and morphology of surface pores can be controlled in a wide range by simply adjusting the content of carbon quantum dots and polymer solution concentration. The effect of the crystallization ability of the adsorption layer formed at the interface between the polymer solution and the water droplet on the pore dimension is proposed. Furthermore, PLLA can be completely degraded by CQD carboxyl catalysis. In brief, we have successfully manufactured a biocompatible PLLA/CQD membrane with a surface porous microstructure which may be potentially applied in cell culture and cell-related research. Compared to previous literature, this work develops a new one-step template patterning method with dynamic controllability. This work also avoids limitations such as high humidity, flowing airflow, and amphiphilic surfactants compared to traditional Breath-Figure approaches.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly (l-lactic acid) membranes
Carbon quantum dots
Porous surface
Breath-Figures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and property of CQD nanoparticles
Influence of CQD solution on membrane surface morphology
Crystallization and thermal degradation of PLLA/CQD membranes
Influence of solution concentration on membrane surface morphology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of carbon quantum dot preparation process. The carbon source was first decomposed and carbonized by microwave radiation. Then the CQDs solution was obtained by oxidation stripping through strong acid hydrothermal reaction. Finally, the CQD solution was obtained by vacuum filtration.
Schematic for the fabrication process of PLLA/CQD porous membranes.

Properties of CQD solution produced by two carbon sources maize straw and rice hull, a1, b1, c1, and the blue line represents quantum dots prepared from rice hull. The size distribution of CQD (a1 and a2), TEM (b1 and b2), and SEM (c1 and c2) observations of the morphology of CQD, (d) FTIR spectrum of CQD, (e) UV–vis absorption, (f) fluorescence spectrum of CQDs in 360 nm.

SEM observations of surface porous structural evolution and pore size distribution of PLLA/CQD membranes. The CQD solution content of (a), (b), (c) is 1 ml, 2 ml, and 3 ml, respectively. The (d) is sample (a) observed by laser scanning confocal microscope.
SEM observations of surface morphology evolution of PLLA membranes with ethanol/water as nonsolvent. The nonsolvent content of (a), (b), (c) is 1 ml, 2 ml and 3 ml, respectively.

DSC curves of PLLA/CQD membranes with different CQD content. (A) is non-isothermal crystallization of PLLA/CQD membranes with different CQD content. The CQD content of (a), (b), (c) is 1 ml, 2 ml, and 3 ml, respectively. (B), (C) and (D) are isothermal crystallization of PLLA/CQD membranes with different CQD content under different temperature. The CQD content of (B), (C) and (D) is 1 ml, 2 ml, and 3 ml, respectively. Temperature is differentiated by color and the curves of black, pink, purple and cyan represent 110 ℃, 120 ℃, 130 ℃ and 140 ℃, respectively.

A and B were TGA and derivative curves of PLLA/CQD membranes with different CQD content. C and D were TGA and derivative curves of PLLA membranes with different ethanol/water content. The content of (a), (b), (c) is 1 ml, 2 ml and 3 ml, respectively.

SEM observations of surface porous structural evolution and pore size distribution of PLLA/CQD membranes. The solution concentration of (a), (b), (c) and (d) is 0.01 g/ml, 0.03 g/ml, 0.07 g/ml and 0.10 g/ml, respectively. The CQD solution content is 2 ml.

head.ce:title: Construction of porous poly (l-lactic acid) surface via carbon quantum dots-assisted static Breath-Figures method

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Laboratory of Yantai Advanced Materials and Green Manufacturing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Focused on the design and construction of surface porous morphology of poly (L-lactic acid) membrane. A bottom-up surface patterning technique, static Breath-Figures method, combined with Pickering emulsion mechanism is regulated to fabricate porous poly (L-lactic acid) membrane surface rapidly and reliably. Carbon quantum dots, synthesized in an environment-respecting route, are used to assist the formation of droplet templates. The dimensions and morphology of surface pores can be controlled in a wide range by simply adjusting the content of carbon quantum dots and polymer solution concentration. The effect of the crystallization ability of the adsorption layer formed at the interface between the polymer solution and the water droplet on the pore dimension is proposed. Furthermore, PLLA can be completely degraded by CQD carboxyl catalysis. In brief, we have successfully manufactured a biocompatible PLLA/CQD membrane with a surface porous microstructure which may be potentially applied in cell culture and cell-related research. Compared to previous literature, this work develops a new one-step template patterning method with dynamic controllability. This work also avoids limitations such as high humidity, flowing airflow, and amphiphilic surfactants compared to traditional Breath-Figure approaches.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw data required to reproduce these findings will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: To construct micropatterned surfaces, traditional lithographic techniques such as soft lithography [1], e-beam lithography [2,3], and nanoimprint lithography [4] have been widely adopted to produce micro/nano-sized patterns of polymers. However, the up-bottom replication and transformation process disadvantages a complicated process, higher equipment requirements, and more expensive costs [5]. In recent years, the concept of self-assembly has been increasingly introduced into patterning research. With the help of these templates, such as colloidal particles and emulsion droplets [6–8], ordered porous materials with a size ranging from a dozen to several dozen nanometers can be constructed[9,10]. This kind of template patterning technology based on the self-assembly process has the advantages of lower cost, easy operation, more substantial versatility, etc., but there are two general disadvantages: one is that the size and shape of the template are fixed; the other is the traditional template not reusable [11–13]. So how developing a new one-step template patterning method with dynamic controllability and broad applicability is significant [14].

Breath-Figure (BF) method is a representative bottom-up surface patterning technique for fabricating micro/nanoscale ordered porous structures [15]. Unlike the previous template method, the template medium used in the Breath-Figure method is non-toxic and readily available water. In addition, the Breath-Figure method avoids the tedious steps of the conventional template method and can realize the one-step preparation process [16–18]. However, plentiful physical factors were reported as responsible for the formation of the Breath-Figures pattern, such as water miscibility, interfacial tensions, the thermodynamic affinity between polymer and solvent, and so on [19,20]. When the polymer solution or other materials dissolved in volatile solvent was placed in a humid airflow or another humidity environment, the rapid volatilization of the solvent will lead to a rapid decrease in the solution surface temperature, which could be dropped to −6 °C. The moisture in the environment quickly condenses into tiny water droplets on the solution’s surface when it cools [21–23]. Therefore, an essential prerequisite for obtaining a porous structure is to use an organic solvent that is volatile and immiscible with water, such as Chloroform and Carbon disulfide. Besides, the water droplets condensed on the solution surface form a uniform size through the nucleation and growth process. The packing density of the water droplet array also increases gradually with the growth of the water droplets. This fast nucleation and slow growth mechanism ensure the uniformity of droplet size [24]. At the same time, the droplet array is arranged into a hexagonal close-packed array with the lowest free energy under the combined action of the convection force caused by the temperature difference between the top and bottom of the liquid membrane and the capillary force between the droplets [25], that is, a honeycomb arrangement.

For all this, the dimensional uniformity of the water droplet is also determined by the stability of the polymer solution and finally determines the surface morphology. Unfortunately, uniform surface pore morphology was difficult to obtain by controlling ambient humidity in previous work [26], since the shape of water droplets could not be effectively maintained. Therefore, how stabilizing the surface water droplet morphology is one of the most critical factors for the preparation of uniform surface pores. Theoretically speaking, the isolation and encapsulation of water droplets can be enhanced by increasing the rigid groups and the molecular chain length of film-forming material. Moreover, small molecule surfactants can also stabilize water droplets by changing their surface tension. However, introducing polar groups or extending molecular chain lengths is difficult, and introducing surfactants can cause contamination, which leads to a narrow application range. Some linear polymers, such as PLLA, have the characteristics of no branching, low solution viscosity, and poor encapsulation of water droplets, that cannot form ordered porous structures by themselves [27–30]. Ideal porous structures can be constructed by adding small amphiphilic components as stabilizers of droplet templates [31]. At present, the preparation of various particle-stabilized emulsions (called Pickering emulsions) using colloidal particles instead of surfactants has attracted extensive attention [32]. After many water droplets are formed on the surface of the polymer, the water droplets form a special water-in-oil emulsion environment on the surface of the solution. The colloidal particles dispersed in the organic solvent are driven by reducing the free energy of the system. Self-assembly of colloidal particles occurs at the liquid-liquid interface and built an adsorption layer. Fusion between emulsion droplets is prevented due to the steric hindrance effect of this adsorption layer [33]. However, the preparation process of most nanoparticles is complicated, and it is easy to introduce toxic substances and other defects, which limit the application of this method. Therefore, it is of great research significance to prepare green nanoparticles in a simple way to assist the construction of porous surfaces.

In this research, a porous PLLA surface is constructed by the CQD-assisted static Breath-Figure strategy without using any surfactants. Samuel et al. used a polymer solution containing a small amount of water in THF, which was miscible at first, but phase separation resulted in the formation of small water droplets at the final stage of THF evaporation. Subsequently, the addition of surfactant to the added water results in selective enrichment of surfactant molecules at the droplet interface, leaving surfactant molecules on the inner pore surface once the water is removed [25]. However, the solvent THF has a certain hydrophilicity to make it mix well with water, which limits the selectivity of the solvent. The addition of surfactants can have an impact on film performance and be difficult to remove at a later stage. Compare with this work, this system has the following notable features. The CQD nanoparticles are synthesized in an environmentally friendly way and used as stabilizers in water droplets according to the Pickering emulsion mechanism. Based on the advantage of the good dispersion of CQD particles and can be mutually soluble with the polymer-solvent, anhydrous ethanol was selected as the solvent for preparing the CQD solution. The CQD solution is added into PLLA film-forming solution and rapidly stirred to produce a polymer emulsion containing CQD, to disperse the hydrophilic CQD nanoparticles in the PLLA solution. Afterward, the Breath-Figure strategy is used for pore formation. Due to poor compatibility with an organic solvent, the CQD nanoparticles in the system will spontaneously move to the surface of the solution-driven by interface energy and density difference. During the nucleation and growth of water droplets, the rapid evaporation of solvent reduces the surface temperature of the solution, and water molecules in the air condense into water droplets on the surface of the polymer solution. The CQD on the surface of the solution spontaneously enters the water-oil interface and stabilizes the water droplets. With the further volatilization of solvent, the viscosity of the polymer gradually fixed the morphology of the pore. These droplets would eventually evaporate leading to the formation of porous polymer membranes. This study elucidates the role of CQDs in polymer-prepared surface porous membranes and establishes a new particle-assisted respiration patterning template method. The present work successfully manufactured a biocompatible PLLA/CQD membrane with a surface porous microstructure. Porous polymer materials prepared by biodegradable polymers have potential applications as scaffolds for cell culture and are the focus of tissue engineering research [34]. Tissue engineering requires cells to adhere to the surface of the scaffold during culture. The high surface area of porous polymer material attracts the culture of small volume and high-density cells. PLLA, a biocompatible degradable polymer, has been widely used in the field of biomedical materials [35]. Some recent studies on the culture of cells on PLLA substrates demonstrate that degradation rate of substrate [36], mechanical properties and bioactivity [37], and the good interfacial bonding with additive [38], strongly affect the adhesion, proliferation, and differentiation of the cultured cells. The CQD nanoparticles, a natural nanomaterial, have attracted increased attention in antibacterial, cancer therapy biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery [39–42]. Combining all the advantages, the membrane could potentially apply in cell culture and cell-related research.

The maize straw and rice hull were used as the carbon source, and all agricultural and sideline products were collected from the field. Poly (L-lactic acid) (PLLA) under the trademark of 4032D, purchased from Natureworks, has a weight average molecular weight of 11.9 × 104. Chemical reagents of analytical grade, including concentrated sulfuric acid, anhydrous ethanol, and chloroform, were purchased from Chuandong Chemical Company (China, Sichuan). All chemical reagents can be used directly without further purification. Deionized water is homemade in the laboratory.

The Carbon Quantum Dots (CQD) was produced by an environment-respecting route. Compared with other methods, this method has the advantages of low energy consumption, simple operation, and a friendly environment. The maize straw or rice hull were first decomposed and carbonized by microwave radiation, and then oxidized and stripped by the strong acid hydrothermal reaction to obtain CQDs solution. The specific operation is shown in Fig. 1. Firstly, the carbon source (1 g) was soaked in 60 ml of dilute H2SO4 solution (0.01 g/ml), followed by ultrasonic dispersion for 20 min to obtain a uniform suspension. Afterward, the suspension was placed in an 800 W microwave oven to radiant heating for 5 min, sequentially poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction mixture was cooled down to ambient temperature, and filtered to obtain the CQD solution. The surface of the prepared CQDs contains many hydroxyl groups and has excellent water solubility, which is convenient to stabilize the water droplets on the membrane surface. The proposed strategy of " trash-to-treasure " opens new possibilities for the high-value utilization of maize straw, which has great potential in the environmental field.

PLLA/CQD porous membranes were prepared through a simple CQD-Assisted Static Breath-Figures Method. The parameters that need to be adjusted are the amount of CQD solution and polymer concentration under the condition of constant 60% humidity. The effects of these parameters on droplet size and separation distance were carefully studied. The detailed preparation process is shown in Fig. 2. First, the CQD solution, which selects maize straw as a carbon source, needs to be diluted with anhydrous ethanol before membrane preparation. The solvent of the CQD solution prepared by the hydrothermal reaction is water. 1 ml CQD solution was evenly poured into 9 ml anhydrous ethanol and ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min. The CQD nanoparticles can be uniformly dispersed in ethanol/water mixed solvent. Then, a certain quality PLLA particle was dissolved in chloroform under continuous stirring to form a homogeneous solution. The solution concentration was controlled at 0.01 g/ml, 0.03 g/ml, 0.05 g/ml, 0.07 g/ml, and 0.10 g/ml, respectively. Subsequently, a certain amount of CQD/ethanol/water mixed solution was added to the PLLA solution and stirred at 600 rpm for 30 min. The volume of the CQD/ethanol/water mixed solution was controlled at 1 ml, 2 ml, and 3 ml, respectively. The solution transforms from transparent to white emulsion. Therewith, 5 ml mixed solution was cast in a glass Petri dishes (diameter: 60 mm) uniformly and moved into a homemade humidity box at 30 °C for 24 h to get PLLA/CQD porous films. Finally, the PLLA/CQD porous film was moved into a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 4 h to dry. To evaluate the standard conditions for the preparation of breath figures, the conditions were optimized using pure PLLA solution mixed with ethanol/water mixed solution without CQD nanoparticles.

The microstructure of CQD and PLLA/CQD porous membranes were analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta FEG250) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. The CQD solution was dropped onto the conductive adhesive and dried with a heat lamp. The PLLA/CQD porous membranes were cut into strips and pasted on the sample table. All samples were sprayed with gold 20 s to improve electrical conductivity. The morphology of CQD was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, ZEISS Gemini 300). Droplets of a suspension of CQD in water were deposited on the ultra-thin carbon film and allowed to dry under ambient conditions before TEM imaging. The surface group of CQD was measured by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet IS50) with a wave number ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1. The UV–visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CQD were measured by UV–visible spectrometer (Shimadzu, UV-1800) and Steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Industries, FLS1000). The size distribution of CQD was measured using Nano-particle size and Zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano, ZS90). Aperture distribution was statistically obtained using Nano Measurer 1.2 software analysis from the SEM images of the fracture surface. The distribution of CQD on pore wall was analyzed under transmitted light (bright field or dark field) and reflected light using a confocal laser microscope (CLSM, Carl Zeiss LSM900). The light path was firstly switched as transmitted light (bright field or dark field) and the eyepiece was chosen to 5X. Then, the PLLA/CQD porous membrane was placed on the sample stage by tuning the focal length to find the focal plane. finally, the membrane was observed by increasing the magnification (10 X, 20 X, 50 X) and changing the reflected light. The crystallization condition is determined by Differential scanning calorimetry (TA instrument, Q20). The degree of crystallinity was obtained by measuring the enthalpy of fusion. The effect of CQD on the crystallization properties of PLLA was studied by isothermal crystallization and non-isothermal crystallization. Samples of about 6 mg were first weighed and sealed in an aluminum pan. The non-isothermal crystallization selection was heated from 30° to 200°C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. The isothermal crystallization selection was first heated quickly to 200 °C and held for 5 min to remove all crystals. Then the temperature was decreased from 200 °C to a certain temperature at a rate of 10 °C/min and kept at a constant temperature for 20 min. Finally, the temperature was increased to 200 °C and the next cycle is started. The constant temperatures were 110 °C, 120 °C, 130 °C, and 140 °C, respectively. The degree of crystallinity is calculated according to the equation as follows (1)
(1)
X
c
=
∆
H
m
∆

are melting enthalpy and melting enthalpy required for the complete crystallization, and
∆

m
0
of PLLA was 93.7 J/g.

The thermal decomposition behavior is determined by Thermogravimetric (TA Instruments, Q50). Samples of about 10 mg were first weighed and sealed in a ceramic crucible. Then the selection was heated from room temperature to 500 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and property of CQD nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The CQD nanoparticles are synthesized in an environmentally friendly way and used as stabilizers in water droplets according to the Pickering emulsion mechanism. Therefore, the morphology, size distribution, and property of CQD nanoparticles play an important role in the formation of membrane surfaces. Fig. 3. provided insights into the size distribution, morphological characteristics, structural properties, and chemical composition of biomass CQD nanoparticles prepared from maize straw and rice hull. According to Fig. 3a, the CQD nanoparticles are evenly distributed, ranging from 150 nm to 250 nm. The size of straw-derived CQD is larger than that of rice hull-derived CQD, reaching a size of 242 nm. Morphological characterization of CQDs was performed using TEM and SEM. Notably, the shapes of the two CQDs are quite different, straw-derived CQDs are rod-shaped while rice hull-derived CQDs are spherical. The FTIR spectra of CQD nanoparticles were shown in Fig. 3d. It was observed that the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups by identifying H-bonded OH, CO, and CO groups, together with the existence of C–H and conjugated CC. Interestingly, the peak intensity of rice hull-derived CQD in the 1032 cm−1 to 1358 cm−1 range much higher than the intensity of straw-derived CQD. This condition is related to the composition of rice hull. The ash left after rice hull combustion is generally 20% of the mass of rice hull. The main component of rice husk ash is SiO2, the content is as high as 87–97%. Therefore, the strong peak in the 1032 cm−1 to 1358 cm−1 range was attributed to the overlap of the CO bond and SiOSi bond. Fig. 3e shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs displays an intense absorption band at 286 nm and weaker ones in the range of 269–304 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO bond. Specifically, the electronic structure of CQDs can be explained by molecular orbital (MO) theory [43–45]. The π-state of CQDs can be attributed to the aromatic sp2-hybridized carbons in its core [46–48]. The functional group carbonyl-containing a lone electron pair forms a bond with the aromatic sp2-hybridized [49]. The electron will transition from the functional group state to the aromatic rings π* state (n→π*) [50–52]. The surface defects of the carbon dots and the surface CO functional groups connected to the carbon skeleton are co-hybridized to form a surface state. This defective surface state combined with radiation transitions leads to the photoluminescence of carbon dots. Fig. 3f shows the fluorescence spectrum, the emission peaks were centered at 469 nm when the CQDs were excited by the excitation wavelengths of 360 nm and showed green fluorescence under UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CQD solution on membrane surface morphology
ce:section.ce:para: PLLA porous membrane was prepared by CQDs-Assisted static Breath-Figures Method. The PLLA particles dissolved in a volatile solvent were first placed in a humidity environment. Then, the rapid evaporation of solvent results in a rapid decrease of the solution surface temperature. Finally, the moisture in the environment quickly condenses into tiny water droplets on the solution surface. However, the inherent hydrophobicity of PLLA makes it difficult to play a stable protective role at the solvent-droplet interface, resulting in the easy convergence of droplets. Interestingly, a porous PLLA membrane surface can be constructed by adding a small amount of CQD particles. Fig. 4 illustrates a surface porous PLLA membrane prepared with different CQD content. For all the PLLA/CQD membranes, many interconnected micrometers scale pores to form a network structure on the surface. The pore diameter increases from 1.85 µm to 30.59 µm with the increase in CQD content. The average pore size would expectedly depend on both the CQD content as well as on the number of water droplets. Fig. 4(d) shows that the CQD on the pore walls fluoresce light green, indicating that the quantum dots are distributed on the pore walls. After many water droplets are formed on the surface of the polymer, the water droplets form a special water-in-oil emulsion environment on the surface of the solution. The CQD nanoparticles dispersed in the organic solvent are driven by reducing the free energy of the system. Self-assembly of CQD nanoparticles occurs at the solvent-droplet interface and built an adsorption layer. Fusion between emulsion droplets is prevented due to the steric hindrance effect of this adsorption layer. However, the pore size distribution was uneven and formed a semi-open hole when CQD content was 3 ml. This pore morphology is determined by the interfacial tension between the polymer solution and the water droplets [53,54]. Due to the high interfacial tension between water and chloroform, the polymer surrounding the water droplets will be pulled up to encapsulate the water droplet, and eventually, solidify to form a semi-open structure. Further, many nanoscale pores are present in macropores, this suggests that increasing CQD concentration contributes to the formation of a larger interface area, which leads to the formation of smaller pores.

Porous membranes can also be produced using polymer solutions containing small amounts of low-vapor-pressure non-solvent, which is miscible initially but would phase separate at the later stages of the evaporation process. To further verify the role of CQD nanoparticles in the preparation of breath figures, the conditions were optimized in pure PLLA solution mixed with ethanol/water solution without CQD nanoparticles. Chloroform and ethanol are completely miscible, and chloroform evaporates much faster than ethanol. As shown in Fig. 5. Unfortunately, no pores were formed when the ethanol/water solution content was below 3 ml. When ethanol/water solution content is 3 ml, although there are pores on the surface, the size is different, and the distribution is irregular. The chloroform-ethanol solution is unstable and will undergo phase separation at the later stages of evaporation. The lower density of ethanol and water rises to the surface of the solution to form an ethanol/water layer, which prevents the formation of droplet nuclei. The increase of ethanol content reduces the volatilization rate of the solution, prolonging the growth time of water droplets and contributing to the formation of a porous surface. The presence of CQD nanoparticles helps to further stabilize the ethanol emulsion and prevents the formation of the ethanol layer, which is of decisive significance to the formation of a porous surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystallization and thermal degradation of PLLA/CQD membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The crystallization ability of the adsorption layer formed at the interface between the polymer solution and the water droplet directly controls the solidification at the interface [55]. Fig. 6 shows the non-isothermal and isothermal crystallization of PLLA/CQD membranes with different CQD content. The glass transition, cold crystallization, and melting peaks can be observed in the non-isothermal crystallization curve. However, the cold crystallinity was decreased from 10.4% to 0.2%, and the crystallinity was decreased from 26.9% to 8.4% with the increase in CQD content. The isothermal crystallization curve further supports this result. The crystallinity was decreased from 45.5% to 26% at 110 ℃ isothermal crystallization for 20 min with the increase of CQD content. The same trend is observed for isothermal crystallization at other temperatures. It comes mainly from the competition of strong hydrogen bonding interactions between the CQD nanoparticles, which are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, and PLA chains that limits crystal growth. Furthermore, the crystallinity of PLLA was decreased from 39.4% to 19.4% further to 0.3% When the crystallization temperature increased from 120 ℃ to 130 ℃ further to 140 ℃. This indicated that high crystallization temperature also inhibits crystal growth. The effect of CQD on the crystallization properties of PLLA further reveals the principle that the surface size increases with the increase of CQD content. The crystallization capacity of the interface is weak, and it cannot be solidified quickly, leading to the fusion of adjacent water droplets.

The thermal degradation property of PLLA and PLLA/CQD membranes was performed to investigate the consequence of CQD nanoparticles on the degradation performance of PLLA membranes [56]. As shown in Fig. 7, the degradation temperature and maximum degradation rate were slightly increased with the increasing CQD nanoparticles content. However, the degradation rate and temperature of PLLA membranes prepared with different non-solvent content did not change significantly. Compared with pure PLLA membranes, the residual carbon and degradation temperature of PLLA/CQD membranes are lower, indicating that CQD nanoparticles can promote the degradation of PLLA membranes. During degradation, PLA first depolymerizes to form oligomers and monomers. However, a dense carbon layer will form on the surface to inhibit further degradation of the inner oligomers as the temperature increases further. Therefore, complete degradation of PLA cannot be achieved through thermal degradation. Interestingly, the surface of the CQDs inside the carbon layer contains carboxyl groups, which can catalyze the degradation of oligomers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of solution concentration on membrane surface morphology
ce:section.ce:para: As an important parameter of the film-forming liquid composition, the polymer solution concentration directly affects the size and morphology of the porous membrane, because it can change the motility of molecular chains and restrict the convergence of water droplets [57,58]. As shown in Fig. 8, the average pore size decreased from 5.78 µm to 2.36 µm, the shape of the pore is gradually transformed from an ellipse to a circle, and the pore wall gradually thickens. The variations in the dimensions and morphology with prominent concentration dependence. The surface pore size is mainly determined by the water droplet size, and the water droplet nucleation stage is the dominant factor determining the water droplet size. The seldom polymer molecule at low concentrations results in poor ability to stabilize droplets and less restriction on droplet growth. The droplet nucleus size formed by high concentration is relatively small, and the subsequent droplet growth process is not affected by solution concentration. Therefore, the pore size formed by the high concentration solution is smaller. The micropores morphology mainly depends on the surface tension between polymer and water droplets and the repulsive force between adjacent water droplets. When the surface tension plays a dominant role, circular micropores are formed, while the repulsive force is dominant, irregular micropores are formed [59]. This phenomenon has been reported in the evolution of bubbles in lipid monolayers [60]. In addition, the solution viscosity increases with concentration, and the excessively high viscosity prevents the water droplets from sinking further into the membrane, so only a single layer of pores can be formed on the surface. Therefore, it is evident that both the CQD content and the solution concentration determine the size of pores formed.

This research successfully constructed a porous PLA surface via Carbon Quantum Dots-Assisted Static Breath-Figures Method. The first is to convert agricultural waste into high-value CQDs in an environmentally friendly way. The excellent dispersibility of CQDs in water, provided by abundant surface oxygen-containing functional groups, effectively improves the stability of water droplets on the surface of PLA solution according to the Pickering emulsion mechanism, which effectively promotes the formation of porous structures. The pore diameter increases from 1.85 µm to 30.59 µm with the increase in CQD content and decreases from 5.78 µm to 2.36 µm with the increase in solution concentration. The crystallinity ability of the adsorption layer formed at the interface between the polymer solution and the water droplet can also control the pore diameter by controlling the solidification at the interface. Furthermore， PLLA can be completely degraded by CQD carboxyl catalysis. Compared to previous literature, this work not only has the advantages of lower cost, easy operation, more substantial versatility, but many constraints such as high humidity, flowing airflow, and amphiphilic surfactants have been effectively avoided. Moreover, this study elucidates the role of CQDs in polymer-prepared surface porous membranes and establishes a new particle-assisted respiration patterning template method. Biocompatible PLA as the matrix, the surface microporous membrane prepared by surface modification of natural CQD particles is expected to become the substrate material for cell culture and cell-related research.

Yunfeng Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation. Xin Sun: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Rui Wang: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Hui Tang: Investigation. Lixin Wang: Investigation. Lihui Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Shuhao Qin: Project administration, Methodology,

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52105222), the LICP Cooperation Foundation for Young Scholars (No. HZJJ20-03), and the Key Research Project of Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2021ZD27) are acknowledged for the financial support.
